concept: Fear / The Deep Tightening / Alarm Felt in the Body
description: Fear: the tightening that rises before danger ('pukea'), real or imagined. Named plainly so it can be felt, spoken, and sat beside — the Metta Sutta transmutation had to route around this word's absence; nothing has to again. Its companions: 'shaelo' (brave) and 'thomari' (courage), which mean nothing without it.
sound_symbolism: The hushed 'su' is the held breath, 'ki' the clench itself, and 'ma' the ground still there beneath the feeling. The word contains its own reassurance: the clench is held by something steady.
grammatical_notes: Noun with 'phaelo': 'mia sukima phaelo.' (I feel fear.) With source: 'lopia lue kurisha sukima phaelo.' (The child feels fear of the storm.) Negated for comfort: 'no ma sukima phaelo. mia ha nai.' (Don't be afraid. I am here.)

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Fear observed is fear already loosening; fear unnameable is fear in charge. The word exists so the observing can start.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Most aggression is fear wearing armor. A community that can say 'I feel fear' skips the armor.
